timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

heb.gloss: circumcise ... the foreskin of your heart
heb.paragraph: The command to "circumcise your hearts" (v.4) transforms the physical covenant sign into a spiritual demand. External ritual without internal transformation provokes rather than placates divine wrath. This concept, rooted in Deuteronomy 10:16, anticipates the new covenant promise (31:33) where God himself will write the law on hearts. Heart circumcision is what physical circumcision was always meant to signify.

refs.note: "Circumcise your hearts, therefore, and do not be stiff-necked any longer" — Moses' original command that Jeremiah renews with greater urgency.
Paul teaches that true circumcision is "of the heart, by the Spirit" — the theological trajectory that runs from Deuteronomy through Jeremiah to the new covenant.
"Rend your heart and not your garments" — the same prophetic insistence on internal over external religion.

notes.note: The conditional "if" signals that return is still possible — but the conditions are demanding: remove detestable idols, swear truthfully by the LORD. Half-measures will not suffice.
Circumcision of the heart demands spiritual transformation matching the external covenant sign. Without it, God's wrath "will break out like fire" — uncontrollable and consuming.
The trumpet blast and signal mark the transition from conditional mercy to announced judgment. "Disaster from the north" — Babylon is coming.

Jeremiah protests: "You have completely deceived this people." This may refer to false prophets who promised peace (cf. 6:14, 8:11), or it may be Jeremiah's anguished response to the gap between prophetic promise and present catastrophe.

Calvin insists that heart circumcision is not a human achievement but a divine work that humans are commanded to seek. The paradox is deliberate: God commands what only God can accomplish, because the command itself drives us to dependence on grace.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase ʿorlat lĕbabkem ("foreskin of your hearts") is a metaphor for spiritual insensitivity — the heart is "uncovered" but not yet responsive to God. The surgical metaphor implies pain is necessary for covenant renewal.

Some scholars emend the text to read "the false prophets have deceived" rather than "you [God] have deceived." The MT reading, however, reflects Jeremiah's raw, unedited protest to God — a feature of prophetic complaint literature.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom reads vv.1–4 as the conclusion of the covenant lawsuit begun in chapter 2. The final "if ... then" structure gives the accused one last chance before sentencing. When the conditions are not met, the rest of chapter 4 delivers the sentence.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann observes that the demand for heart circumcision exposes the insufficiency of every external reform — including Josiah's. The problem is not behavior but interiority. No institutional program can reach the level of transformation that God requires.

hist.context: Chapter 4 opens with a final conditional offer of mercy (vv.1–4) before the tone shifts decisively to judgment. The "if" clauses of vv.1–2 represent the last off-ramp before the highway of destruction. When the trumpet sounds in v.5, the time for decision has passed. The battle alarm language ("Blow the trumpet! Raise the signal!") reflects military emergency procedures — the enemy is at the gates.

sections.header: Verses 11–22 — "A Scorching Wind from the Barren Heights"

heb.paragraph: God declares: "My people are fools (ʾĕwîlîm); they do not know me" (v.22). The root ʾĕwîl in wisdom literature denotes not intellectual deficiency but moral-spiritual obtuseness — the refusal to acknowledge reality. Israel's "foolishness" is willful ignorance of God, a theme linking Jeremiah to Proverbs and Ecclesiastes.

refs.note: Isaiah similarly describes the approaching enemy with terrifying speed and military precision — the prophetic tradition shares a vocabulary of invasion.
Jeremiah's personal anguish in 4:19 anticipates the fuller lament of 8:18–9:1, where he becomes the "weeping prophet" — personally devastated by the judgment he announces.

notes.note: The "scorching wind" is not the gentle breeze used for winnowing grain but a sirocco — the devastating desert wind that destroys everything. This wind comes as judgment, not purification.

Jeremiah's personal lament reveals the emotional cost of prophetic ministry. He does not stand aloof from the disaster he announces but shares the agony of his people. The trumpet of war is physically painful to the prophet.
God's assessment: "My people are fools; they do not know me." The verb "know" (yāda') is the same used for intimate relationship — Israel's fundamental failure is relational, not merely behavioral.

Calvin praises Jeremiah's pastoral heart: a true prophet does not delight in announcing judgment but suffers with those he condemns. The pain is genuine, not rhetorical — Jeremiah models the compassion that should characterize all who speak God's hard truths.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The word rûaḥ can mean "wind" or "spirit." In context, the "scorching wind" is both a natural phenomenon (the sirocco) and a metaphor for divine judgment — God's rûaḥ as destroyer rather than life-giver.
The Hebrew mēʿay mēʿay ("my bowels, my bowels") reflects the ancient understanding that the intestines were the seat of deep emotion. Modern translations render this as "my anguish" or "my heart."

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom traces the progressive approach of the enemy through geographic markers: first from the desert (v.11), then from Dan in the far north (v.15), then from a distant land (v.16), and finally surrounding Jerusalem itself (v.17). The narrowing circle creates mounting dread.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies a collision between the prophetic voice and the divine voice. In v.19, Jeremiah cries out in anguish; in v.22, God speaks with cold assessment. The two voices do not harmonize — the prophet cannot reconcile his love for the people with God's verdict against them.

hist.context: The imagery intensifies: a scorching desert wind (not the gentle winnowing breeze) is coming — too violent for any useful purpose, purely destructive. The invader approaches "like clouds," with chariots "like a whirlwind" and horses "swifter than eagles." The rapid-fire similes create a sense of overwhelming, unstoppable force. Jeremiah's own emotional response breaks through in v.19: "Oh, my anguish! I writhe in pain!"

sections.header: Verses 23–31 — "I Looked at the Earth, and It Was Formless and Empty"

heb.paragraph: Jeremiah uses the identical phrase from Genesis 1:2 — tōhû wābōhû, "without form and void." The coming judgment is depicted as creation in reverse, an un-creation that returns the land to primordial chaos. Mountains quake, birds flee, the fruitful land becomes desert. This is not mere military defeat but cosmic catastrophe — sin undoes the very fabric of created order.

refs.note: The phrase tōhû wābōhû ("formless and void") appears only here and in Genesis 1:2. Jeremiah deliberately echoes the creation narrative to portray judgment as un-creation.
Zephaniah's vision of total sweeping away — humans, animals, birds, fish — similarly describes judgment as reversal of the creation order.
Paul's vision of creation groaning in travail reflects the same theology: human sin has cosmic consequences, and redemption must be equally cosmic in scope.

notes.note: Four "I looked" (rāʾîtî) statements present judgment as the reversal of creation: (1) earth — formless and void; (2) heavens — no light; (3) mountains — quaking; (4) people — gone. The sequence systematically undoes Genesis 1 in reverse order.
Yet "I will not destroy it completely" — even within the un-creation vision, God sets a limit to judgment. Total annihilation is reserved; a remnant will survive.

Jerusalem personified as a woman in labor, screaming "Woe to me!" The image of a woman adorning herself for lovers who will murder her is a graphic portrayal of Judah's fatal miscalculation in courting foreign alliances.

Calvin notes that Jeremiah's use of Genesis 1:2 language teaches that sin returns the world to primordial chaos. What God ordered, sin disorders. The prophetic vision reveals the true metaphysical weight of covenant-breaking — it threatens the very structure of reality.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase tōhû wābōhû occurs only twice in the Hebrew Bible: Genesis 1:2 and here. Its reuse is clearly intentional, creating an intertextual link that frames judgment as cosmic de-creation.

The qualifying phrase "yet I will not destroy completely" (kālâ lōʾ ʾeʿĕśeh) is a crucial theological limitation. Even in the most extreme prophetic rhetoric, total destruction is never the final word.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies the fourfold "I looked" (rāʾîtî) as a visionary report — the prophet describes what he sees in a divine revelation. The structure mirrors creation's "and God saw" (wayyarʾ) in Genesis 1, now inverted: the prophet sees un-creation.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann calls this "the most radical rhetorical act in the book" — the poet dares to imagine the end of the world. The vision refuses all comfortable categories: this is not reform, not discipline, not correction — it is the undoing of everything. Only from such radical negation can genuinely new creation emerge.

hist.context: The "un-creation" vision of vv.23–26 is one of the most theologically dense passages in the Hebrew Bible. Jeremiah sees the coming destruction not as an isolated historical event but as a reversal of Genesis 1. The four "I looked" (rāʾîtî) statements systematically dismantle creation: earth becomes chaos, heavens lose their light, mountains shake, humanity vanishes. The only parallel in the prophetic literature is the apocalyptic visions of Isaiah 24 and Zephaniah 1.

themes.note: Judgment peaks at 10 — the un-creation vision and military invasion imagery are the most intense in the book so far. Sovereignty at 8 reflects God's control over both creation and its reversal.

lit.note: The chapter moves from conditional mercy (vv.1–4) through military alarm (vv.5–18) to cosmic vision (vv.23–28) and personal lament (vv.29–31). The un-creation passage (vv.23–26) is the theological climax — judgment as reversal of Genesis 1.

This concept, rooted in Deuteronomy 10:16, anticipates the new covenant promise (31:33) where God himself will write the law on hearts. Heart circumcision is what physical circumcision was always meant to signify.

God declares: "My people are fools (ʾĕwîlîm); they do not know me" (v.22). The root ʾĕwîl in wisdom literature denotes not intellectual deficiency but moral-spiritual obtuseness — the refusal to acknowledge reality. Israel's "foolishness" is willful ignorance of God, a theme linking Jeremiah to Proverbs and Ecclesiastes.

Jeremiah uses the identical phrase from Genesis 1:2 — tōhû wābōhû, "without form and void." The coming judgment is depicted as creation in reverse, an un-creation that returns the land to primordial chaos. Mountains quake, birds flee, the fruitful land becomes desert. This is not mere military defeat but cosmic catastrophe — sin undoes the very fabric of created order.

ppl.name: Jeremiah
ppl.text: Jeremiah protests: "You have completely deceived this people…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Disaster from the North

translations.text: Raise the signal to go to Zion! Flee for safety without delay! For I am bringing disaster from the north, even terrible destruction.”
Raise a standard toward Zion, flee for safety, stay not, for I bring disaster from the north, and great destruction.
This is what the Lord says to the people of Judah and to Jerusalem: “Break up your unplowed ground and do not sow among thorns.
For thus says the LORD to the men of Judah and Jerusalem: “Break up your fallow ground, and sow not among thorns.

rec.quote: "The conditional "if" signals that return is still possible — but the conditions are demanding: remove detestable idols, swear truthfully by the LORD. Half-measures will not suffice."

debate: Some scholars emend the text to read "the false prophets have deceived" rather than "you [God] have deceived." The MT reading, however, reflects Jerem…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Disaster from the North within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: "Circumcise your hearts, therefore, and do not be stiff-necked any longer" — Moses' original command that Jeremiah renews with greater urgency.
Paul teaches that true circumcision is "of the heart, by the Spirit" — the theological trajectory that runs from Deuteronomy through Jeremiah to the new covenant.
"Rend your heart and not your garments" — the same prophetic insistence on internal over external religion.
Isaiah similarly describes the approaching enemy with terrifying speed and military precision — the prophetic tradition shares a vocabulary of invasion.
Jeremiah's personal anguish in 4:19 anticipates the fuller lament of 8:18–9:1, where he becomes the "weeping prophet" — personally devastated by the judgment he announces.
The phrase tōhû wābōhû ("formless and void") appears only here and in Genesis 1:2. Jeremiah deliberately echoes the creation narrative to portray judgment as un-creation.

coaching.tip: 'Circumcise your hearts' (v. 4). Physical circumcision marked covenant membership; Jeremiah demands the internal reality behind the external sign. This phrase reappears in Deuteronomy 30:6 and Romans 2:29 — Paul's argument that true circumcision is 'of the heart, by the Spirit.' Jeremiah stands midway between Moses and Paul, insisting the sign is worthless without the substance.

'I looked at the earth, and it was formless and empty' (v. 23) — tohu wabohu, the exact Hebrew phrase from Genesis 1:2. Jeremiah sees judgment as uncreation: the world reverting to pre-creation chaos. Sin doesn't just break rules; it unmakes the world. The vocabulary choice is precise and devastating — what God built in Genesis, disobedience can unbuild.
